risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients with RRMS were randomized in the BEYOND trial (NCT00099502) to receive either IFNB-1b 250 or 500 µg every other day or glatiramer acetate (GA) 20 mg daily in a 2:2:1 ratio using block randomization with regional stratification [25]." Comment: The study explicitly reports block randomization with regional stratification, which indicates a systematic random allocation process and is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random for this domain.

Quote: "Patients with RRMS were randomized in the BEYOND trial (NCT00099502) ... in a 2:2:1 ratio using block randomization with regional stratification [25]." Comment: The report describes the randomization sequence (block randomization/stratification) but gives no explicit information about how the allocation was concealed from the recruiters/enrolling clinicians. No central allocation, sealed opaque numbered envelopes, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is stated, so foreknowledge of the next assignment cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Treating physicians and patients were aware of allocation to IFNB or GA, but were blinded to IFNB dose." Comment: Participants and the personnel delivering/monitoring treatment were not blinded to whether the patient received IFNB-1b versus GA. Because the key outcomes in this review include relapse and disability worsening, knowledge of treatment assignment could plausibly influence co-interventions, symptom reporting, and clinical management, so performance bias is high overall.

Quote: "Evaluating physicians were blinded to all treatment assignments [25]." Comment: For the key clinical outcomes in this trial, outcome assessment was performed by evaluating physicians who were explicitly blinded, which lowers the chance that knowledge of allocation could influence assessment. Although depression as an adverse event was also recorded by treating physicians who were not blinded, the main trial outcomes listed (relapse and disability worsening) are largely clinician/evaluator assessed and the report states the evaluators were blinded. The self-report BDI-II depression scale is patient-completed, so it is not an assessor-blinding issue for that measure; the detection-bias risk for the trial outcomes reported here is therefore low.

Quote: "A total of 24 patients did not receive treatment with study medication and accordingly were omitted from the per-protocol analysis." Comment: This indicates essentially complete follow-up for the randomized cohort (2244 randomized; 24 not treated), with no signal of substantial or imbalanced post-randomization missingness described for the trial outcomes. The paper also notes that BDI-II data were available for a substantial subset and were similar across arms: "Only six patients eligible for BDI-II testing did not perform a screening assessment" and at study end "BDI-II scores were provided by 794 patients." Although the depression substudy had incomplete data, the authors state the proportion with BDI data was similar in each treatment group, and there is no evidence that missingness was differential or related to outcome in a way likely to bias the comparisons.
